sanskrit_devanagari:
अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन् ।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया ॥ ६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
ajo 'pi sann avyayātmā bhūtānām īśvaro 'pi san |
prakṛtiṁ svām adhiṣṭhāya sambhavāmy ātma-māyayā || 6 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Although I am unborn and My transcendental body never deteriorates, and although I am the Lord of all living entities, by My internal energy I still appear in every millennium in My original transcendental form.
Although I am unborn and My transcendental body never deteriorates, and although I am the Lord of all living entities, I still appear in every millennium in My original transcendental form.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 4436 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord has spoken about the peculiarity of His birth: although He may appear like an ordinary person, He remembers everything of His many, many past “births,” whereas a common man cannot remember what he has done even a few hours before.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.6 Api, san ajah, though I am birthless; and avyayatma, undecaying by nature, though I am naturally possessed of an undiminishing power of Knowledge; and so also api san, though; isvarah, the Lord, natural Ruler; bhutanam, of beings, from Brahma to a clump of grass; (still) adhisthaya, by subjugati…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's statement of the apparent paradox: the unborn and imperishable Lord appears to take birth, but only by his own māyā. Māyā here is the divine creative power, not illusion in the negative sense — Krishna's appearance is real-seeming but not bound by the causal chain that produces ordinary births.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.6 Without forsaking any of the My special qualities, as supreme rulership, birthless, imperishability etc., I am born by My free will. Prakrti means one’s own nature. The meaning is that by employment of My own Nature and taking a form of My choice, I incarnate by My own will (Maya).
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as central: the Lord, who is by nature unborn and imperishable and the controller of all beings, presides over his own prakṛti (his own divine body, śuddha-sattva nature) and incarnates through his own divine will (ātma-māyā as divine self-disposing). The avatar's body is real and divine, not illusory.

positions.rendering: I appear in My original transcendental form (through internal potency)
By my own māyā (divine creative power); the appearance is not bound by ordinary causation
Through divine self-disposing — the avatar body is genuinely divine (śuddha-sattva), the result of svarūpa-saṅkalpa

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The major fault-line: Shankara permits a reading where the avatar's body is māyā in a sense closer to 'apparent form'; Ramanuja insists the avatar's body is real and divine, not māyic in any reducing sense. Prabhupada follows the Vishishtadvaita-Gaudiya reading. This is one of the most theologically loaded verses in the chapter.

literal_meaning: Though unborn and imperishable in nature, though Lord of beings, presiding over my own prakṛti, I come into being through my own māyā.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna states the avatar doctrine for the first time. He is unborn (aja) and imperishable (avyayātmā), the Lord (īśvara) of beings — yet, presiding over his own prakṛti (creative power), he manifests through his own māyā (yogic self-disposing power). The verse holds two simultaneous claims: ontologically Krishna does not undergo birth in the way embodied beings do; phenomenally he appears to. The mechanism is ātma-māyā — his own creative power, not external constraint.
